Title: H2S-mediated protein S-sulfhydration modulates infectivity and autophagy in the rice blast fungus

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S)-mediated S-sulfhydration state affecting autophagy
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: post-translational sulfur signaling / autophagy-related metabolic regulation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Controls autophagic homeostasis, appressorium maturation, and fungal pathogenicity
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: S-sulfhydration of ATG18 at Cys78 promotes PtdIns(3)P binding and restrains autophagy; mutation C78A weakens binding, accelerates autophagy, improves appressorium function, and increases virulence
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ATG18 is an in vivo and in vitro S-sulfhydrated target protein in M. oryzae, with Cys78 as the critical modification site. 2. C78A mutation reduces appressorium collapse, accelerates autophagy, and increases pathogenicity. 3. S-sulfhydration-induced structural changes alter ATG18 stability and phospholipid binding. 4. ATG18 binds strongly to PtdIns(3)P, and this binding is regulated by the C78 sulfhydration state.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: WD40 repeat protein; seven-bladed β-propeller autophagy protein
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Wild type shows punctate cytoplasmic localization; C78A mutant is predominantly vacuolar; exogenous H2S attenuates punctate localization
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: NaHS enhances S-sulfhydration and affects localization/binding; nitrogen starvation induces autophagy
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: proteomics; tag-switch assay; site-directed mutation (C78A); complementation; LC-MS/MS site identification; PIP strip binding assay; molecular docking; molecular dynamics simulation; GFP-Atg8 autophagy assay; western blot; pathogenicity assay
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: C78A mutation increases virulence and accelerates autophagy, which could enhance infection capacity
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ATG18 sulfhydration machinery is a candidate antifungal target; promoting excessive fungal H2S/S-sulfhydration may disrupt autophagy and infection
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ATG18 is not a transcription factor or catalytic pathway enzyme in this study; it functions as an autophagy effector whose lipid binding is modulated by S-sulfhydration. The C78 site is conserved across many fungi

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Deletion of DES1 does not affect H2S production or virulence in M. oryzae
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DES1 was tested as another candidate H2S pathway gene in M. oryzae. 2. DES1 expression is downregulated during infection relative to mycelia. 3. DES1 deletion does not alter fungal growth, H2S production, or pathogenicity. 4. DES1 is dispensable for virulence in this study.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Downregulated during infection stage compared with mycelial stage
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene knockout; complementation; expression analysis; H2S measurement; pathogenicity assay
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Low priority target for antifungal engineering because it was dispensable for H2S production and virulence in this fungus
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although a sulfur-metabolism gene, DES1 was not supported as a major functional determinant of the phenotype compared with CSE1

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Regulates fungal autophagy, appressorium maturation, redox homeostasis, and infectivity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces H2S; CSE1 overexpression elevates H2S and total protein S-sulfhydration, inhibits autophagy and reduces virulence, whereas deletion lowers H2S and increases virulence
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CSE1 (MGG_10380) is the key H2S-synthesizing enzyme in M. oryzae. 2. Overexpression of CSE1 increases H2S and global protein S-sulfhydration, disrupts autophagy, lowers appressorial turgor, and reduces pathogenicity. 3. Deletion of CSE1 decreases H2S and enhances lesion formation and invasive growth. 4. CSE1 localizes mainly to the cytoplasm and is active across developmental stages.
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: H2S biosynthesis from sulfur amino acid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Cysteine/homocysteine degradation branch linked to H2S generation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point; identified as the crucial H2S synthase for virulence-associated signaling
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes degradation of cysteine and homocysteine with release of hydrogen sulfide during sulfur amino acid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expression declines during appressorium development stages (AP_3H and AP_12H)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High cytoplasmic GFP-CSE1 signal across developmental stages
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: NaHS treatment elevates H2S and S-sulfhydration; hypotaurine lowers H2S; nitrogen starvation used for autophagy assays
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene knockout; complementation; overexpression; qRT-PCR; AzMC H2S imaging; tag-switch assay; pathogenicity assays; metabolite measurement
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native promoter for complementation; ptripc constitutive promoter for overexpression
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: CSE1 overexpression causes ROS accumulation, lower GSH/GSSG ratio, defective appressorial turgor, impaired invasive growth, and reduced virulence
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Targeting fungal CSE1/H2S signaling or applying H2S donors may suppress fungal infection; fungal-specific inhibition of this pathway is a potential disease-control strategy
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: DES1 deletion did not alter H2S production or virulence, highlighting CSE1 as the dominant H2S synthase in this fungus
